# Declaration Precedes Governance

## The Physics

A thing that has not been declared does not exist as far as governance
is concerned. It may exist physically — code on disk, a system in
production, a product being shipped — but it is invisible to the
governance system, invisible to discovery agents, and invisible to
anyone navigating the fleet.

Declaration is the act of making a thing **visible to the navigation
layer**. It is not optional metadata; it is the precondition for
every governance operation that follows. Specifically:

- **You cannot enforce a rule against an undeclared thing.** The rule
  has no target to bind to, no path to traverse, no field to check.
- **You cannot discover an undeclared thing.** Agents (LLM or human)
  walking the fleet stop at the boundary of what is declared. What's
  beyond is dark space.
- **You cannot assemble around an undeclared thing.** Higher-order
  systems built on top of components depend on those components being
  declared and addressable. Undeclared components are unassemblable.
- **You cannot reason about the health of an undeclared thing.** Health
  surfaces aggregate from declared sub-things. Hidden sub-things are
  outside the aggregation.

This is not a moral claim — it's a structural one. Declaration is the
mechanism by which a thing enters the governable, discoverable, and
assemblable space. Everything else flows downstream of it.

## Why It Matters for JOS

JOS is positioned as the meta-OS that other operating systems run on.
For ventures to adopt JOS, for agents like Atlas to work with the
fleet, for cross-venture assembly to be possible, every governed
thing must declare itself in a standardized way. Without that uniform
declaration surface:

- Agents waste effort searching for things that aren't where they're
  expected to be
- Governance can only check what it can find (so non-compliance hides
  in invisible corners)
- The fleet drifts because individual ventures decide their own
  declaration shape, and cross-fleet navigation collapses
- New ventures don't know what to declare, so they declare nothing,
  and the navigation surface gets thinner over time

The declaration surface is the substrate every other JOS mechanism
depends on. When it's complete and uniform, everything above it works.
When it's patchy, everything above it works patchily.

## The Four-Step Ladder

Once a thing is declared, it climbs a ladder that JOS-P24 ("Declared,
Exemplified, Guided, Governed") formalizes:

1. **Declared** — the thing's existence is named, located, identifiable
2. **Exemplified** — a canonical instance or template shows what good
   looks like
3. **Guided** — documentation or a playbook tells readers how to engage
4. **Governed** — a binding rule, contract, or standing order enforces
   the commitment

The order matters: you cannot exemplify what hasn't been declared, you
cannot guide engagement with what isn't exemplified, and you cannot
govern adherence to what isn't guided. The ladder ascends; skipping
rungs collapses the structure.

## Connected Objects

- **JOS-P24** — Declared, Exemplified, Guided, Governed (the principle this concept anchors)
- **JOS-SO-015** — Declaration Required for Governable Things (the operational rule)
- **JOS-FW-001** — Holonic Venture Decomposition Framework (declaration is how each level of the holon becomes visible)
- **JOS-C01** — Fractal Self-Similarity (declaration recurses at every level)
- **JOS-D0049** — Universal Base Manifest (declaration cascade root)
- **JOS-D0052** — Governance Discovery Standard (declaration is the precondition for discoverability)

## Academic / Industry Backing

The principle that systems must be observable to be governable is
load-bearing in:

- **Control theory** — a system cannot be controlled if its state
  cannot be observed (observability is a precondition for controllability)
- **Software architecture** — the dependency-inversion principle and
  interface-segregation principle both presuppose that components have
  declared, addressable interfaces
- **Organizational design** — Conway's Law applies in reverse:
  organizational structure becomes visible only at the boundaries
  where teams declare their interfaces to each other
- **Documentation systems** (Diataxis, llms.txt, etc.) — modern AI-era
  documentation patterns assume documents declare their type so agents
  can route to them correctly

The recurring observation across these fields: **the visible surface
is the governable surface**.
